Title: Altered metabolite accumulation in tomato fruits by coexpressing a feedback-insensitive AroG and the PhODO1 MYB-type transcription factor

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Phenylpropanoid; aromatic amino acid; volatile benzenoid/phenylpropanoid
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fruit peel and flesh at red ripe stage
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: DAHPS, shikimate-pathway entry enzyme; feedback-insensitive bacterial mutant enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Shikimate pathway / aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Entry step supplying chorismate-derived aromatic amino acids and downstream phenylpropanoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream; first committed entry step of shikimate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses phosphoenolpyruvate and erythrose 4-phosphate to form DAHP, increasing flux through the shikimate pathway
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for phenylalanine and tyrosine, thereby feeding downstream phenylpropanoid and aromatic volatile synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a critical metabolic bottleneck / key control step in the shikimate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: In tomato fruit, AroG strongly increases phenylalanine and tyrosine and elevates several Phe-derived volatiles; when coexpressed with PhODO1, it further boosts nonvolatile hydroxycinnamate-related metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgene expressed in fruit; analyzed in red ripe fruit tissues
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse-grown plants; ripe red fruit stage; coexpression with PhODO1 in AO F1 lines
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in tomato; genetic crossing with PhODO1 line; qRT-PCR; LC-MS metabolite profiling; GC-MS volatile analysis; amino acid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AroG expression alone greatly increased phenylalanine and tyrosine in tomato fruit. 2. AroG increased several Phe-derived volatiles including phenylacetaldehyde, 2-phenylethanol, 1-nitro-2-phenylethane and eugenol. 3. AroG alone had limited effects on most nonvolatile phenylpropanoids, indicating precursor supply alone is insufficient for broad downstream accumulation. 4. In combination with PhODO1, AroG enhanced accumulation of caffeic acid-, coumaric acid- and ferulic acid-derived metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful to raise aromatic amino acid supply and enhance flavor/nutritional phenylpropanoids; likely most effective when stacked with regulatory genes such as PhODO1 or other phenylpropanoid-activating MYBs
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Alone had little effect on fruit ripening; in coexpression lines the ODO1-associated ripening delay remained; increased precursor supply may be redirected away from volatiles when downstream nonvolatile demand is high
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The expressed form is feedback-insensitive to phenylalanine inhibition. The paper states this bypasses normal feedback control and reveals DAHPS as a bottleneck. Source species and applied species differ and should not be merged.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics by UPLC/qTOF-MS; targeted volatile profiling by GC-MS/GC-FID; no accession reported

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Nonvolatile phenylpropanoids; hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives; aromatic volatiles; phenylalanine; tyrosine
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Phenylpropanoid; aromatic amino acid; volatile benzenoid/phenylpropanoid
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Fruit peel and flesh at red ripe stage
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant and flavor-associated metabolites; nutritional quality improvement
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Phenylalanine/tyrosine precursor synthesis and downstream nonvolatile phenylpropanoid biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Genes encoding most steps for phenylalanine and tyrosine synthesis; multiple PHENYLALANINE AMMONIA LYASE (PAL) isoforms; multiple downstream phenylpropanoid biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates/up-regulates expression of aromatic amino acid biosynthetic genes and multiple PAL and downstream phenylpropanoid genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major switch redirecting metabolic flux from free phenylalanine and aromatic volatiles toward nonvolatile phenylpropanoids, especially hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PhODO1 overexpression decreases free phenylalanine, increases tyrosine, strongly increases many nonvolatile phenylpropanoids, retards fruit maturation/ripening, and in AO with AroG promotes especially high hydroxycinnamate accumulation while reducing many flavonoid and volatile branches
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transgene expressed in fruit; line 8117 had highest expression and was used for crossing; analyzed in red ripe fruit tissues
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse-grown plants; ripe red fruit stage; coexpression with AroG in AO F1 line
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in tomato; qRT-PCR; LC-MS metabolite profiling; GC-MS volatile analysis; comparison of ODO1, AroG, AO, and wild type fruits
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PhODO1 overexpression strongly reprogrammed tomato fruit phenylpropanoid metabolism. 2. It lowered free phenylalanine but increased tyrosine, consistent with enhanced downstream consumption of phenylalanine. 3. It promoted accumulation of many nonvolatile phenylpropanoids, especially ferulic acid-related compounds, while reducing naringenin- and some quercetin-derived metabolites and not increasing most Phe-derived volatiles. 4. In combination with AroG, it drove even stronger accumulation of caffeic acid-, coumaric acid- and ferulic acid-derived metabolites.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable regulator for engineering antioxidant hydroxycinnamates in tomato fruit; can be stacked with precursor-supply genes like AroG, though ripening delay and branch-specific reduction of some flavonoids/volatiles should be considered
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Retards fruit maturation/ripening; depletes free phenylalanine; reduces many naringenin-, quercetin- and aromatic volatile metabolites due to flux diversion toward nonvolatile phenylpropanoids
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper relies partly on prior evidence that ODO1 elevates expression of shikimate/aromatic amino acid genes and PAL isoforms in tomato. In this study ODO1 is a principal causal regulator of flux redistribution.
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics by UPLC/qTOF-MS; targeted volatile profiling by GC-MS/GC-FID; no accession reported
